title: AGENTS.md — using ggOceanMaps with an AI assistant
text: basemap() returns regular ggplot object. map projected (polar custom CRS), decimal-degree data must transformed first — otherwise points land wrong place: unprojected (decimal-degree) maps can use geom_point() directly x = lon, y = lat.
code: # Use transform_coord() to project decimal-degree data dt <- transform_coord(my_df, bind = TRUE)   # adds lon.proj / lat.proj basemap(data = my_df) +   geom_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj))  # Or use ggspatial::geom_spatial_*, which handles the transform internally basemap(limits = c(-20, 30, 50, 70)) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(data = my_df, aes(x = lon, y = lat),                                 crs = 4326)

title: AGENTS.md — using ggOceanMaps with an AI assistant
text: limits order c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax), min/max pairs. c(-20, 30, 50, 70) “lon −20 30, lat 50 70”. single number means polar projection bounded latitude. Projected maps need transform_coord() added data. Polar maps custom-CRS maps decimal degrees. Using geom_point(aes(x = lon, y = lat)) polar basemap places points wrong location. transform_coord(data, bind = TRUE) adds lon.proj lat.proj columns; plot . use ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(). Antimeridian crossing: limits like c(160, -160, 60, 80) work, use rotate = TRUE get sensible-looking map. Without rotate, package prints message shows whole world. Bathymetry styles need data. bathy.style = "rcb", "pb", "cb" require ggOceanMapsLargeData download (options(ggOceanMaps.datapath)). "rub" / "rug" require options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "path//file.nc"). default bathymetry = TRUE uses bundled binned raster works without download. Don’t add + coord_sf() manually. basemap() already sets coord_sf() right projection limits. Adding another reset package usually clip map incorrectly. shapefiles vs shape: shapefiles (plural) selects underlying spatial dataset ("Arctic", "Europe", …). shape ggplot2 aesthetic marker shape. Easy confuse. First call may take . bathymetry = TRUE triggers download, first basemap slow. Subsequent calls reuse cached data.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: basemap() (R/basemap.R) main user-facing function. follows pipeline: Determine bathy style — parses bathy.style string internal command name via define_bathy_style() Detects input “case” (polar limits, decimal degree limits, projected limits, sf/sp data, etc.) via basemap_data_detect_case() Resolves pre-made shapefile set use via define_shapefiles() / shapefile_list() Loads/downloads spatial data via load_map_data() Clips shapefiles map extent via basemap_data_crop() Computes grid line geometries via basemap_define_grid_lines() Assemble ggplot2 layers — map_cmd() (R/map_cmd.R) returns ggplot2 command strings element (base, land, glaciers, bathymetry, grid definitions). concatenated + executed via eval(parse(text = layers)). Annotate output — returned ggplot object gets custom attributes (bathymetry, glaciers, limits, polarmap, crs, etc.) attached downstream use. qmap() (R/qmap.R) thin wrapper basemap() quickly plotting data frame’s coordinates.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Three automatic projections selected define_shapefiles() (R/define_shapefiles.R): - EPSG:3995 — Arctic Polar Stereographic (max latitude ≥ 60, min ≥ 30) - EPSG:3031 — Antarctic Polar Stereographic (max latitude ≤ −60, min ≤ −30) - EPSG:4326 — Decimal degrees (everything else) Rotation across antimeridian handled rotate_crs() / dd_clip_boundary() R/internal_functions.R.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: shapefile_list() (R/shapefile_list.R) registry pre-made shapefile sets: ArcticStereographic, AntarcticStereographic, DecimalDegree, Svalbard, Europe. entry specifies CRS, limits, paths land, glacier, bathymetry objects. Built-low-res data (dd_land, dd_rbathy) ship package /data/. High-res data downloaded ggOceanMapsLargeData getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath") (defaults tempdir(); users set permanent path .Rprofile via .ggOceanMapsenv$datapath). load_map_data() (R/load_map_data.R) handles existence checks, interactive download prompts, object loading.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: transform_coord() (R/transform_coord.R) — transforms decimal degree coordinates map projection use geom_* layers guess_coordinate_columns() (R/guess_coordinate_columns.R) — infers columns hold lon/lat column names auto_limits() (R/auto_limits.R) — derives map limits data frame

title: Adding graphical elements
text: two common situations: velocity field (u/v components grid) want quiver plot, hand-drawn schematic arrows (“Figure 1” style arrows summarise general circulation region).

what: A velocity field as a quiver plot
title: Adding graphical elements
text: pattern : thin grid drawable density, scale arrow length, draw geom_segment(). example uses small synthetic field runs without download.  projected map axes metres, transform start points add velocity projected units: field lives NetCDF, read stars::read_stars(), .data.frame() , rename coordinate/velocity columns, thin df[seq(1, nrow(df), = n), ] steps .
code: set.seed(1) grd <- expand.grid(lon = seq(-15, 25, by = 4),                    lat = seq(52, 68, by = 2)) grd$u <- 0.6 * cos(grd$lat / 8)          # eastward component (m/s) grd$v <- 0.3 * sin(grd$lon / 6)          # northward component (m/s)  scale <- 2                                # degrees drawn per m/s -- tune to taste grd$lon_end <- grd$lon + grd$u * scale grd$lat_end <- grd$lat + grd$v * scale  basemap(limits = c(-20, 30, 50, 70)) +   geom_segment(     data = grd,     aes(x = lon, y = lat, xend = lon_end, yend = lat_end),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")),     colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.4   ) # transform_coord adds lon.proj / lat.proj in the map's CRS (metres) grd <- transform_coord(grd, proj.out = 3995, bind = TRUE)  scale_m <- 1e5                            # metres drawn per m/s grd$lon_end_proj <- grd$lon.proj + grd$u * scale_m grd$lat_end_proj <- grd$lat.proj + grd$v * scale_m  basemap(60) +   geom_segment(     data = grd,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, xend = lon_end_proj, yend = lat_end_proj),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")),     colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.4   )

title: Adding graphical elements
text: publication-style arrows summarising circulation region, Barents-Sea-currents repository distributes ready-made, editable arrows Barents Greenland Seas. arrows stored small set nodes (decimal degrees) grouped per arrow, type column ("Atlantic" / "Arctic"). nodes sparse purpose (easy edit); run X-spline arrow smooth plotting. recipe reproduces classic two-colour current map ggOceanMaps basemap: Key points: CSV decimal degrees; transform_coord(..., proj.= 3995) moves Arctic-stereographic CRS basemap() uses region. decimal-degree map skip transform map aes(x = long, y = lat) directly. graphics::xspline(..., shape = -0.6, draw = FALSE) returns smoothed coordinates without drawing anything — exactly feed geom_path(). Smooth projected coordinates (transform_coord()), degrees, curvature matches map. cite arrows, see citation source repository.
code: # 1. Read the editable node table (decimal degrees) cur <- read.csv(paste0(   "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MikkoVihtakari/",   "Barents-Sea-currents/master/tabular/barents_currents.csv" ))  # 2. Transform the nodes to the map projection (Arctic stereographic, metres) cur <- transform_coord(cur, lon = "long", lat = "lat",                        proj.out = 3995, bind = TRUE)  # 3. Smooth each arrow with an X-spline through its nodes. #    xspline() needs an open graphics device even with draw = FALSE, so #    open a null device for the calculation. smooth_arrow <- function(d) {   grDevices::pdf(NULL); on.exit(grDevices::dev.off()); graphics::plot.new()   xs <- graphics::xspline(d$lon.proj, d$lat.proj, shape = -0.6, draw = FALSE)   data.frame(lon.proj = xs$x, lat.proj = xs$y,              group = unique(d$group), type = unique(d$type)) } cur_smooth <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(cur, cur$group), smooth_arrow))  # 4. Plot, mapping arrow colour to current type basemap(limits = c(0, 50, 68, 83), shapefiles = "Arctic") +   geom_path(     data = cur_smooth,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, group = group, colour = type),     linewidth = 0.6,     arrow = arrow(type = "open", angle = 15, ends = "last",                   length = unit(0.3, "lines"))   ) +   scale_colour_manual(     name = "Current type",     values = c("Arctic" = "blue", "Atlantic" = "red")   )

title: Adding graphical elements
text: frequent request small pie chart station summarising composition (species proportions, water-mass fractions, …). cleanest way ggplot2 map scatterpie package, whose geom_scatterpie() draws one pie per row data frame set value columns. data frame needs position (x, y), optional radius, one column per pie slice:
code: install.packages("scatterpie") pies <- data.frame(   lon  = c(5, 15, 25),   lat  = c(56, 62, 68),   cod  = c(40, 10, 70),   haddock = c(35, 30, 20),   saithe  = c(25, 60, 10) ) slices <- c("cod", "haddock", "saithe")

title: Adding graphical elements
text: Plot directly longitude/latitude. r (pie radius) degrees , unit map axes:
code: library(scatterpie)  basemap(limits = c(-5, 35, 52, 72)) +   geom_scatterpie(     data = pies,     aes(x = lon, y = lat, r = 1.5),   # radius in degrees     cols = slices,     colour = NA, alpha = 0.9   ) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Species")

title: Adding graphical elements
text: Transform pie centres first, give r projected unit (metres polar projections):
code: pies <- transform_coord(pies, proj.out = 3995, bind = TRUE)  basemap(60) +   geom_scatterpie(     data = pies,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, r = 1.5e5),   # radius in metres     cols = slices,     colour = NA, alpha = 0.9   ) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Species")

what: Scaling pies by a total and adding a size legend
title: Adding graphical elements
text: Map radius variable (e.g. total catch) add radius legend geom_scatterpie_legend(): Tips: Keep colour = NA (slice borders) small pies; borders dominate map scale. geom_scatterpie() adds fill aesthetic, composes separate fill scale ggOceanMaps uses bathymetry add pies bathymetry. need two independent fill scales (e.g. bathymetry pie slices) use ggnewscale’s new_scale_fill() layers. Choose radius unit map axes: degrees decimal-degree map, metres projected map. radius looks right one map invisible (fill plot) .
code: pies$total <- rowSums(pies[, slices]) pies$r <- 0.4 + 1.6 * pies$total / max(pies$total)   # degrees  basemap(limits = c(-5, 35, 52, 72)) +   geom_scatterpie(     data = pies,     aes(x = lon, y = lat, r = r),     cols = slices, colour = NA, alpha = 0.9   ) +   geom_scatterpie_legend(pies$r, x = -2, y = 70, n = 3) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Species")

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: Substitute g final b abbreviation get grey variant (rbb → rbg, wemb → wemg, …). full alias list see ?basemap.

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: package bundles coarse global bathymetry raster (dd_rbathy) ’s always available. default — just toggle bathymetry = TRUE:  set style explicitly:  Greyscale variant:  shipped raster intentionally coarse package stays CRAN’s size cap. ’s fine overview maps quick exploratory plots; publication maps ’ll usually want one higher-detail options .
code: basemap(limits = c(-20, 30, 50, 70), bathymetry = TRUE) basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rbb") basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rbg")

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: companion repository ggOceanMapsLargeData hosts higher-resolution rasters, polygon-contour bathymetries, contour lines supported regions (Decimal Degree, Arctic Stereographic, Antarctic Stereographic, plus several pre-made regional shapefile sets). basemap() downloads needs first use caches locally. One-time setup. Pick permanent directory (files don’t vanish R sessions) add line ~/.Rprofile: high-res style download directory reuse : first call menu asks confirm download (~15–100 MB depending region). Subsequent calls instant.
code: options(ggOceanMaps.datapath = "~/ggOceanMaps_data") # Continuous raster — recommended for most maps basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rcb")  # downsample = n speeds up rendering at the cost of detail basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rcb", downsample = 10)  # Greyscale variant basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rcg")  # Polygon contours (pre-2.0 default) basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "pb") basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "pg")  # Plain contour lines basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "cb") basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "cg")

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: Sometimes want specific dataset — latest GEBCO grid, ETOPO 2022 variant, regional IBCAO compilation, custom-processed file group. Download file , point ggOceanMaps ggOceanMaps.userpath, use rub / rug styles: file format stars::read_stars() can open accepted (NetCDF .nc, GeoTIFF .tif, GMT .grd, …). file read fully every time, user-raster route can slower pre-processed ggOceanMapsLargeData object region. Use downsample trade resolution speed. ggOceanMaps.userpath can also used get_depth() look point depths raster:
code: # Set once (in .Rprofile for persistence): options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc")  # Then any basemap call uses your file: basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rub") basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rub", downsample = 10) basemap(c(11, 16, 67.3, 68.6), bathy.style = "rug") dt <- data.frame(lon = seq(-20, 80, length.out = 41), lat = 50:90) dt <- get_depth(dt, bathy.style = "ru") qmap(dt, color = depth) + scale_color_viridis_c()

what: 4. Live download from a Web Coverage Service (WCS)
title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: one-maps workflows don’t want manage local files, ggOceanMaps can fetch bathymetry demand two OGC Web Coverage Services. pre-download needed; downloaded tiles cached getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath") subsequent calls bbox instant. pick EMODnet area outside European coverage, get clear error pointing ETOPO: (Replace wemb wceb call works.)
code: # North Sea — high-resolution European waters from EMODnet basemap(c(2, 3, 54, 55), bathy.style = "wemb")  # Hawaii — global coverage from ETOPO basemap(c(-160, -154, 18, 23), bathy.style = "wceb")  # Indonesia / Java Trench — outside EMODnet, also ETOPO basemap(c(110, 120, -20, 30), bathy.style = "wceb") basemap(c(110, 120, -20, 30), bathy.style = "wemb") #> Error: Bounding box (110.0° to 120.0° lon, -20.0° to 30.0° lat) lies #> entirely outside the approximate coverage of EMODnet (≈-36° to 43° lon, #> 15° to 90° lat). #> For global bathymetry coverage, download GEBCO or ETOPO data locally and #> use raster_bathymetry() + vector_bathymetry() instead, or wait for a #> global WCS source to be added to ggOceanMaps.

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: Decimal-degree limits . Polar maps projected-CRS limits supported (yet). Per-source size caps. EMODnet defaults 50 deg² maximum bounding box (reads 8-byte doubles internally 4° tile already exceeds read cap); ETOPO defaults 2000 deg² underlying grid much coarser. Override either max_area_deg2 = ... wcs_bathymetry(). Larger boxes tiled mosaicked automatically. Citation. EMODnet CC-(https://emodnet.ec.europa.eu/en/bathymetry); ETOPO1 Amante & Eakins 2009, NOAA NGDC (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/etopo-global-relief-model). Cite source publishing figures.

title: Bathymetry in ggOceanMaps
text: need contour polygons, depth bins, matched land + bathymetry pair, raster_bathymetry() / vector_bathymetry() / vector_land() pipeline turns raster source reusable shapefile objects: depths = NULL skips binning step gives continuous raster — useful geom_raster-style fills without vectorization overhead. Save processed objects don’t pay processing cost :
code: # 1. Process the raster — crop, sign-flip, optionally bin into contours rb <- raster_bathymetry(   "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = c(50, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000),  # depth break points   boundary = c(-5, 10, 50, 60)                  # crop to your region )  # 2a. Vectorize bathymetry into depth-band polygons vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)   # drop islands < 10 km²  # 2b. Vectorize land from the same raster vl <- vector_land(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)  # 3. Plot — both layers plug into basemap() basemap(c(-5, 10, 50, 60),         shapefiles = list(land = vl, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),         bathymetry = TRUE) save(vb, vl, file = "my_region_bathy.rda")

what: How do I plot the Arctic / Antarctic?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: single value limits means “polar projection bounded latitude”:
code: basemap(60)    # Arctic basemap(-60)   # Antarctic

what: How do I plot a region that crosses the antimeridian?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: Without rotate = TRUE ’ll get whole world message; , ggOceanMaps rotates projection antimeridian sits middle.
code: basemap(limits = c(160, -160, 60, 80), rotate = TRUE)

what: How do I fit the map to my data automatically?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
code: dt <- data.frame(lon = c(-150, 150), lat = c(60, 85)) basemap(data = dt, rotate = TRUE)

what: How do I add points to a decimal-degree map?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
code: dt <- data.frame(lon = c(0, 10, 20), lat = c(55, 60, 65)) basemap(limits = c(-10, 30, 50, 70)) +   geom_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red", size = 3)

what: How do I add points to a projected map?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: Projected maps (polar, custom CRS) need coordinates transformed first: let ggspatial handle transform:
code: dt <- data.frame(lon = c(0, 10, 20), lat = c(70, 75, 80)) dt <- transform_coord(dt, bind = TRUE)   # adds lon.proj, lat.proj basemap(60) +   geom_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj),              color = "red", size = 3) basemap(60) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),                                 crs = 4326, color = "red", size = 3)

what: How do I quickly plot a data frame’s coordinates?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook

what: How do I use my own bathymetry raster (e.g. GEBCO, ETOPO, IBCAO)?

what: How do I fetch bathymetry on demand from a web service?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: Two WCS sources wired basemap() — pick region: manual fetch (e.g. pass vector_bathymetry() custom shapefile workflow), call wcs_bathymetry() directly: Requirements / caveats: Decimal-degree limits ; polar maps supported. EMODnet covers European regional seas (≈−36° 43° lon, ≈15° 90° lat). Outside , use ETOPO. ggOceanMaps error clear message suggested alternative pick wrong source. Bbox size capped per source: 50°² EMODnet (~115 m grid), 2000°² ETOPO (~1.85 km grid). Override max_area_deg2 larger requests; function tiles automatically. Caching uses getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath") (tempdir() fallback). Set permanently .Rprofile cross-session reuse. Citation: EMODnet CC-(https://emodnet.ec.europa.eu/en/bathymetry); ETOPO1 Amante & Eakins 2009, NOAA NGDC (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/etopo-global-relief-model).
code: # North Sea — high resolution from EMODnet basemap(c(2, 3, 54, 55), bathy.style = "wemb")  # Hawaii — global coverage from ETOPO basemap(c(-160, -154, 18, 23), bathy.style = "wceb")  # Indonesia / Java Trench — also ETOPO (outside EMODnet) basemap(c(110, 120, -20, 30), bathy.style = "wceb") bathy_emo <- wcs_bathymetry(c(2, 3, 54, 55),   source = "emodnet") bathy_eto <- wcs_bathymetry(c(110, 120, -20, 30), source = "etopo")  basemap(c(110, 120, -20, 30),         shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL,                           bathy = bathy_eto$raster),         bathymetry = TRUE)

what: How do I make land + bathymetry from a downloaded raster?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: full pipeline single source raster (GEBCO, ETOPO, IBCAO, …) matched land + bathymetry pair : Use depths = NULL instead depth vector keep raster continuous grid (skips vectorization, faster rendering): Tip: save processed objects .rda files don’t re-process full raster every session:
code: library(ggOceanMaps)  # 1. Process the raster — crop, sign-flip, bin depth levels rb <- raster_bathymetry(   "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = c(50, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000),  # depth contour break points   boundary = c(-5, 10, 50, 60) )  # 2a. Vectorize bathymetry to depth-polygon shapefile vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)   # drop islands < 10 km²  # 2b. Vectorize land from the same raster vl <- vector_land(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)  # 3. Plot — plug both into basemap() basemap(   limits = c(-5, 10, 50, 60),   shapefiles = list(land = vl, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),   bathymetry = TRUE ) rb_cont <- raster_bathymetry("path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",                              depths = NULL, boundary = c(-5, 10, 50, 60))  basemap(limits = c(-5, 10, 50, 60),         shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL, bathy = rb_cont$raster),         bathymetry = TRUE) save(vb, vl, file = "north_sea_bathy.rda")

what: How do I draw ocean current vectors over a map?
title: ggOceanMaps cookbook
text: pattern : load velocity NetCDF, thin grid drawable density, transform coordinates projected map, draw arrows geom_segment(). projected (polar) map coordinates must transformed plotting (map axes metres, degrees):
code: library(stars) library(dplyr)  # 1. Load u/v current components from a NetCDF uv <- read_stars(c("u_current.nc", "v_current.nc"))  # 2. Convert to a data frame and thin to a manageable density df <- as.data.frame(uv) |>   na.omit() |>   filter(row_number() %% 5 == 0)   # keep every 5th grid point  names(df)[1:4] <- c("lon", "lat", "u", "v")  # 3. Scale arrow length so they look good on the plot scale <- 0.5   # degrees per m/s — tune to taste df <- df |>   mutate(lon_end = lon + u * scale,          lat_end = lat + v * scale)  # 4. Plot basemap(limits = c(10, 40, 70, 80), bathymetry = TRUE) +   geom_segment(     data = df,     aes(x = lon, y = lat, xend = lon_end, yend = lat_end),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")),     color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.4   ) # transform_coord adds lon.proj / lat.proj columns in the map's CRS df <- transform_coord(df, proj = 3995, bind = TRUE) df <- df |>   mutate(     lon_end_proj = lon.proj + u * scale * 1e4,     lat_end_proj = lat.proj + v * scale * 1e4   )  basemap(75, bathymetry = TRUE) +   geom_segment(     data = df,     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj,         xend = lon_end_proj, yend = lat_end_proj),     arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm")),     color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.4   )

what: Building land + bathymetry from a raster
title: Customising shapefiles
text: single source grid (GEBCO, ETOPO, IBCAO, …) gives matched land bathymetry pair. pipeline raster_bathymetry() first, vector_bathymetry() /vector_land(): raster_bathymetry() returns bathyRaster: list processed raster (stars object) depth intervals. Feed object vectorisers: vector_land() takes bathyRaster vector_bathymetry() cleanup arguments (drop.crumbs, remove.holes, smooth), land bathymetry edges line . keep continuous (unbinned) raster bathymetry instead depth-contour polygons, pass depths = NULL raster_bathymetry() use rb$raster directly bathy (skipping vector_bathymetry()). Save processed layers re-run heavy raster step every session:
code: # 1. Read + crop + sign-flip the grid; bin to depth contours. rb <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = "path/to/your/bathymetry.nc",   depths = c(50, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000),  # contour break points   proj.out = 4326,   boundary = c(-5, 10, 50, 60)                  # crop early to save memory ) # 2a. Depth-contour polygons for the vector bathymetry styles vb <- vector_bathymetry(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)   # drop polygons < 10 km^2  # 2b. Land polygons extracted from the same grid vl <- vector_land(rb, drop.crumbs = 10)  # 3. Plug both into basemap() basemap(   limits = c(-5, 10, 50, 60),   shapefiles = list(land = vl, glacier = NULL, bathy = vb),   bathymetry = TRUE ) save(vb, vl, file = "north_sea_shapes.rda") # later: load("north_sea_shapes.rda")

title: ggOceanMaps User Manual
text: Graticules plotted using ggplot2::coord_sf() regarded panel element ggplot2::theme(). Note also background color included panel element (see next section). ggOceanMaps::theme_map() function, automatically executed together every basemap() qmap() command, controls position graticules set top default:  move graticules layers, use panel.ontop argument ggplot2::theme():  Sometimes need control position graticules layers. time writing, ggplot offer straightforward way , can use code ggplot2::coord_sf() make graticules:  exception polar maps graticules drawn ggOceanMaps (ggplot2 graticules maps bugged years ago writing function; part requires revision point).
code: basemap(limits = c(-20, 15, 50, 70), grid.col = "red", grid.size = 1) basemap(limits = c(-20, 15, 50, 70), grid.col = "red", grid.size = 1) +    theme(panel.ontop = FALSE) p <- basemap(limits = c(-20, 15, 50, 70), grid.col = NA) + # without graticules    geom_sf(data = ices_areas, aes(fill = SubArea), show.legend = FALSE)   # Make the graticules: lims <- attributes(p)$limits  graticule <- sf::st_graticule(   c(lims[1], lims[3], lims[2], lims[4]),    crs = attributes(p)$crs,   lon = seq(-180, 180, 15), lat = seq(-90, 90, 5))  # Plot reorder_layers(p) +   geom_sf(data = graticule, color = "grey70", size = LS(0.5)) + # graticules   coord_sf(xlim = lims[1:2], ylim = lims[3:4], # redefine limits            crs = attributes(p)$crs) +   # labels on top of graticules   geom_sf_label(     data = suppressWarnings(sf::st_centroid(sf::st_make_valid(ices_areas))),      aes(label = Area_Full)   ) X <- basemap_data(60)  basemap(60, grid.col = NA) +   geom_sf(     data = X$map.grid$lon.grid.lines,      color = "red", size = 1) +   geom_sf(     data = X$map.grid$lat.grid.lines,      color = "red", size = 1) +   geom_sf(     data = X$shapefiles$land,      fill = "grey60", color = "black", size = 0.1)

title: ggOceanMaps User Manual
text: Similarly, ggplot2::theme() panel element allows coloring ocean:  want graticules top, ’ll need define manually , , background grid, contained ggplot element called panel:
code: basemap(expand.grid(lon = c(-129, -124), lat = c(49, 53)), grid.col = "red", grid.size = 0.5) +    theme(panel.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue"),         panel.ontop = FALSE) p <- basemap(expand.grid(lon = c(-129, -124), lat = c(49, 53)), grid.col = NA) +    theme(panel.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue"),         panel.ontop = FALSE)   # Make the graticules: lims <- attributes(p)$limits  graticule <- sf::st_graticule(   c(lims[1], lims[3], lims[2], lims[4]),    crs = attributes(p)$crs,   lon = attributes(p)$map.grid$lon.breaks,    lat = attributes(p)$map.grid$lat.breaks )  # Plot p +    geom_sf(data = graticule, color = "red", size = LS(1)) + # graticules   coord_sf(xlim = lims[1:2], ylim = lims[3:4], # redefine limits            crs = attributes(p)$crs)

what: Add longitude and latitude labels to a polar map
title: ggOceanMaps User Manual
text: Since adding longitude latitude labels polar maps requires manual fiddling map, premade solution . Instead, can using sf package:
code: library(sf); library(dplyr)  p <- basemap(-60, shapefiles = "Antarctic")  p +    geom_text(     data =       attributes(p)$map.grid$lat.grid.lines %>%        sf::st_coordinates() %>%        as.data.frame() %>%        dplyr::filter(X == -180) %>%       dplyr::add_row(X = -180, Y = -90, L1 = 3, L2 = 1) %>%        transform_coord(proj.out = attributes(p)$crs, bind = TRUE),     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, label = paste0(abs(Y), "\u00B0S"))   ) +   geom_text(     data =       attributes(p)$map.grid$lon.grid.lines %>%        sf::st_coordinates() %>%        as.data.frame() %>%        dplyr::filter(Y == -60) %>%       dplyr::mutate(Y = -63) %>%        transform_coord(proj.out = attributes(p)$crs, bind = TRUE),     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj,          label = ifelse(sign(X) == 1, paste0(abs(X), "\u00B0E"),                         paste0(abs(X), "\u00B0W"))     )   ) +   theme(plot.margin = margin(0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25, "cm"))

title: New features in ggOceanMaps version 2
text: shift stars package allowed efficient plotting raster data. made possible add support raster bathymetries. Consequently, bathymetry system stemmed PlotSvalbard, predecessor ggOceanMaps, redesigned. can always roll back old system setting options(ggOceanMaps.bathy.style = "poly_blues") Rprofile, please read new system better. ggOceanMaps now ships low-resolution raster bathymetry dataset decimal degree coordinate reference system (4326). dataset projected fly depending basemap limits. projection faster quality much worse compared pre-v2.0 ggOceanMaps: Processing time: 1.4 sec low-resolution default bathymetry optimized processing time higher-resolution datasets available ’ll need download . downloads happen automatically, others, ’ll need bit setup work. automatic download, ggOceanMapsLargeData contains compressed version ETOPO 60 arc-second global relief model (’s 60 Arc-Second Resolution/Ice surface_elevation netCDF webpage). Use download dataset simply specifying bathy.style argument: Processing time: 10.6 sec best bit. resolution still enough, can use bathymetry grid want long stars::read_stars can open . First, ’ll download entire GEBCO 15 arc-second grid (click - netCDF link “GEBCO_2023 Grid (ice surface elevation)” table website). dataset 7.5 Gb size, ’ll need good internet connection. Unzip dataset downloads folder, define option either beginning R session Rprofile: options(ggOceanMaps.userpath = "~/Downloads/gebco_2023/GEBCO_2023.nc"). Now can make maps using 15 arc-second GEBCO data: Processing time: 33.6 sec basemap() now downsample argument can used reduce resolution excessively large custom raster datasets given region (lower resolution, smaller file size): Processing time: 38.8 sec Note processing time change case, seems shorter smaller maps. can also use custom bathymetries packages, marmap, ’ll specify correct bathy.style data (improved future versions package):  Note shapefiles providing better polygon bathymetry resolution ggOceanMapsLargeData deprecated now. Remaking shapefiles quality-controlling time consuming since rewrite code quality-control changes. shapefiles options might appear future releases ggOceanMaps. Since new bathymetry system mostly raster based now, advised save maps made ggOceanMaps raster format (journals accept jpegs highest quality setting >= 600 ppi resolution). learn new bathymetry system, please check User Manual
code: basemap(c(100, 160, -20, 30), bathymetry = TRUE) basemap(c(100, 160, -20, 30), bathy.style = "rcb") basemap(c(100, 160, -20, 30), bathy.style = "rub") basemap(c(100, 160, -20, 30), bathy.style = "rub", downsample = 10) library(marmap)  dt <- data.frame(lon = c(-125, -125, -111, -111), lat = c(28, 37, 37, 28)) limits <- auto_limits(dt, expand.factor = 1.1)$projLimits  mar_bathy <- marmap::getNOAA.bathy(   lon1 = limits["xmin"], lon2 = limits["xmax"],    lat1 = limits["ymin"], lat2 = limits["ymax"]) bathy <- raster_bathymetry(stars::st_as_stars(marmap::as.raster(mar_bathy)),                             depths = NULL, verbose = FALSE)  basemap(dt, shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, bathy = bathy), bathy.style = "rcb")

title: SI2022 poster
text: Script download data used poster: Note correct way download something like shown website, user name making poster, <7000 records shark species Northeast Atlantic. Hence 100k limit issue. Citing correct data sources, however, issue planning use data serious applications posters, make sure find correct DOI using occ_download() web portal.
code: # GBIF data  library(tidyverse) library(ggOceanMaps) library(rgbif)  ## Donwload example from: https://docs.ropensci.org/rgbif  species <- c(BlueShark = "Prionace glauca", BaskingShark = "Cetorhinus maximus")  GBIF <- lapply(seq_along(species), function(i) {      message(species[i], "...")      key <- rgbif::name_backbone(name = species[[i]])[c("canonicalName", "speciesKey")]      rgbif::occ_search(     taxonKey = key$speciesKey,      return = "data",      limit = 4e5,      geometry = c(-20, 30, 20, 76)) })  GBIF_blue <- GBIF[[1]]$data %>%    rename("lat" = "decimalLatitude", "lon" = "decimalLongitude") %>%    dplyr::select(lon, lat) %>%    transform_coord(bind = TRUE) %>%    dist2land()  GBIF_basking <- GBIF[[2]]$data %>%    rename("lat" = "decimalLatitude", "lon" = "decimalLongitude") %>%    dplyr::select(lon, lat) %>%    transform_coord(bind = TRUE) %>%    dist2land()  # Dugnad for havet data  DugnadForHavet <- read_delim(   "data/DFH_basking_shark.csv", # from: https://dugnadforhavet.no/dataportal   delim = ";") %>%    arrange(amount) %>%    filter(!is.na(longitude), !is.na(latitude)) %>%    transform_coord(bind = TRUE) %>%    dist2land() %>%    as_tibble() keys <- species %>%    name_backbone_checklist() %>% # match to backbone    filter(!matchType == "NONE") %>% # get matched names   pull(usageKey)  occ_download(   pred_in("taxonKey",key$speciesKey),   pred("hasCoordinate", TRUE),   pred("hasGeospatialIssue", FALSE),   pred_within("POLYGON ((-20 30, 20 30, 20 80, -20 80, -20 30))") )

title: SI2022 poster
code: ## Background map  a1_h <- 841-1 # Poster height without margins (A1) a1_w <- 594-1.2 # Poster width without margins (A1)  asp <- a1_h/a1_w # To get correct size because aspect ratio is fixed lon_min <- -1.5e6 # Projected coordinates lon_max <- 2e6  lat_min <- -6.5e6 lat_max <- lat_min + asp*diff(c(lon_min, lon_max))  bm <-    basemap(     c(lon_min, lon_max, lat_min, lat_max),      shapefiles = "Arctic",     lon.interval = 10,     bathymetry = TRUE,     legends = FALSE) +   theme(axis.title = element_blank()) +   geom_spatial_point(     data = DugnadForHavet,     aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, size = amount),     color = "#FF5F68", alpha = 0.8   ) +   geom_density_2d(     data = DugnadForHavet, contour_var = "ndensity", size = LS(1),     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, color = after_stat(level))   ) +   geom_spatial_point(     data = GBIF_blue %>% filter(ldist > 0),      aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "#FFC95B", alpha = 0.8, size = 3   ) +   geom_density_2d(     data = GBIF_blue, contour_var = "ndensity", size = LS(1),     aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj, color = after_stat(level))   ) +   scale_size(range = c(1,10)) +   scale_color_distiller(palette = "Spectral", na.value = NA) +   theme(legend.position = "none")  ## Distance to land  DugnadForHavet %>%    mutate(species = "Basking shark") %>%    dplyr::select(species, ldist) %>%    bind_rows(     GBIF_basking %>% mutate(species = "Basking shark") %>%        dplyr::select(species, ldist),     GBIF_blue %>%        mutate(species = "Blue shark") %>%        dplyr::select(species, ldist)   ) %>%    ggplot(aes(x = ldist, after_stat(ndensity), color = species)) +    geom_freqpoly(size = LS(5)) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("#FF5F68", "#FFC95B")) +   labs(x = "Standardized density", y = "Distance from land (km)",         color = "Species") +   coord_cartesian(expand = FALSE, ylim = c(0,1.05), xlim = c(0,500)) +   theme_classic() +   theme(legend.position = c(0.7,0.9))

title: Pre-made shapefiles
text: Bathymetry — binned raster shipped package continuous version: Land — Natural Earth land + minor islands. Natural Earth polygons require s2 engine turned clipping: Glaciers — Natural Earth glaciated areas + Antarctic ice shelves:
code: # Binned raster bathymetry (shipped as dd_rbathy) dd_rbathy <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = file.path(etopoPath, "ETOPO_2022_v1_60s_N90W180_surface.nc"),   depths = c(50, 300, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 4000, 6000, 10000),   proj.out = 4326,   downsample = 1 ) save(dd_rbathy, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_rbathy.rda"), compress = "xz")  # Continuous raster bathymetry dd_rbathy_cont <- raster_bathymetry(   bathy = file.path(etopoPath, "ETOPO_2022_v1_60s_N90W180_surface.nc"),   depths = NULL,   proj.out = 4326,   downsample = 1 ) save(dd_rbathy_cont, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_rbathy_cont.rda"), compress = "xz") s2_mode <- sf::sf_use_s2() suppressMessages(sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)) on.exit(suppressMessages(sf::sf_use_s2(s2_mode)))  world   <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_land/ne_10m_land.shp")) islands <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_minor_islands/ne_10m_minor_islands.shp"))  world   <- sf::st_make_valid(rbind(world, islands)) dd_land <- clip_shapefile(world, c(-180, 180, -90, 90)) all(sf::st_is_valid(dd_land))  save(dd_land, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_land.rda"), compress = "xz") glaciers    <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_glaciated_areas/ne_10m_glaciated_areas.shp")) iceshelves  <- sf::st_read(file.path(NEDPath, "ne_10m_antarctic_ice_shelves_polys/ne_10m_antarctic_ice_shelves_polys.shp"))  glaciers   <- sf::st_make_valid(rbind(glaciers, iceshelves)) dd_glacier <- clip_shapefile(glaciers, c(-180, 180, -90, 90)) all(sf::st_is_valid(dd_glacier))  save(dd_glacier, file = file.path(outPath, "dd_glacier.rda"), compress = "xz")

title: Create a ggplot2 basemap for plotting variables — basemap
text: Mikko Vihtakari

title: Create a ggplot2 basemap for plotting variables — basemap
code: # The easiest way to produce a map is to use the limits # argument and decimal degrees:  basemap(limits = 60) # synonym to basemap(60)  # \donttest{ # Bathymetry can be added using the respective argument: basemap(limits = -60, bathymetry = TRUE)   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Glaciers require a download in the new version: basemap(limits = -60, glaciers = TRUE, shapefiles = "Arctic") } # }  # The easiest way to add data on the maps is to use the ggspatial functions: dt <- data.frame(lon = c(-150, 150), lat = c(60, 90)) if(requireNamespace("ggspatial", quietly = TRUE)) { basemap(data = dt, bathymetry = TRUE) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),     color = "red") } #> Assuming `crs = 4326` in stat_spatial_identity()  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Note that writing out data = dt is required because there are multiple # underlying ggplot layers plotted already: basemap(data = dt) + ggspatial::geom_spatial_point(dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat), color = "red") #> Error: `mapping` must be created by `aes()` } # }  # If you want to use native ggplot commands, you need to transform your data # to the projection used by the map: dt <- transform_coord(dt, bind = TRUE)  basemap(data = dt) +   geom_point(data = dt, aes(x = lon.proj, y = lat.proj), color = "red")   # The limits argument of length 4 plots a map anywhere in the world: basemap(limits = c(100, 160, -20, 30), bathymetry = TRUE)   # The limits are further expanded when using the data argument:  dt <- data.frame(lon = c(-160, 160, 160, -160), lat = c(80, 80, 60, 60))  if(requireNamespace("ggspatial", quietly = TRUE)) { basemap(data = dt) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_polygon(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),     fill = NA, color = "red")  # Rotate: basemap(data = dt, rotate = TRUE) +   ggspatial::geom_spatial_polygon(data = dt, aes(x = lon, y = lat),     fill = NA, color = "red") } #> `geom_polypath()` is deprecated: use `ggplot2::geom_polygon()` with the `subgroup` aesthetic #> `geom_polypath()` is deprecated: use `ggplot2::geom_polygon()` with the `subgroup` aesthetic #> Assuming `crs = 4326` in stat_spatial_identity()   # Alternative: basemap(data = dt, rotate = TRUE) +   geom_polygon(data = transform_coord(dt, rotate = TRUE),     aes(x = lon, y = lat), fill = NA, color = "red")   ## To find UTM coordinates to limit a polar map: basemap(limits = 60, projection.grid = TRUE)   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # (Arctic shapes require a download in 2.0) basemap(limits = c(2.5e4, -2.5e6, 2e6, -2.5e5), shapefiles = "Arctic")  # Using custom shapefiles (requires download): data(bs_shapes, package = "ggOceanMapsData") basemap(shapefiles = list(land = bs_land))#'  # Premade shapefiles from ggOceanMapsLargeData (requires download): basemap("BarentsSea", bathymetry = TRUE) } # }  # grid.col = NA removes grid lines, rotate = TRUE rotates northwards: basemap(limits = c(-180, -140, 50, 70), grid.col = NA, rotate = TRUE)   # Rename axis labels  basemap(limits = c(-140, -105, 20, 40), bathymetry = TRUE) + xlab("Lat")   # Remove axis labels  basemap(limits = c(0, 60, 68, 80)) + labs(x = NULL, y = NULL)   basemap(limits = c(0, 60, 68, 80), rotate = TRUE) + theme(axis.title = element_blank(),       axis.text = element_blank(),       axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),       axis.ticks.y = element_blank()       )  # }

Either character argument referring name pre-made shapefiles shapefile_list, single sf sp polygons object object NULL enable automatic definition land shapes based data. text: Mikko Vihtakari

title: Calculate distance to the closest land for coordinates — dist2land
code: # Simple example: dt <- data.frame(lon = seq(-20, 80, length.out = 41), lat = 50:90) dt <- dist2land(dt, verbose = FALSE) # \donttest{ qmap(dt, color = ldist) + scale_color_viridis_c()   # Datasets covering the entire Earth seem to work now, except 0,0 lon/lat point lon = deg_to_dd(seq(0,360,30)); lat = c(80,50,20,0,-20,-50,-80)  dt <- data.frame(  lon = rep(lon, length(lat)), lat = rep(lat, each = length(lon)))  qmap(dist2land(dt, verbose = FALSE), color = ldist) +  scale_color_viridis_c()  # } if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ dt <- data.frame(   lon = deg_to_dd(seq(0,360,length.out = 1e3)),    lat = rep(60, 1000))    # The distance calculation is slow for large datasets system.time(dist2land(dt)) # user  system elapsed  # 12.677   0.146  12.849   # binary = TRUE speeds the function up system.time(dist2land(dt, binary = TRUE)) # user  system elapsed  # 1.239   0.120   1.369  } # }

what: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: Fetches gridded bathymetry data demand OGC Web   Coverage Service (WCS) returns bathyRaster object   compatible basemap vector_bathymetry.   Two sources currently supported: "emodnet" (~115 m European   waters) "etopo" (1 arc-minute / ~1.85 km global).

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
code: wcs_bathymetry(   limits,   source = "emodnet",   coverage = NULL,   cache_dir = NULL,   force = FALSE,   max_area_deg2 = NULL,   tile_size_deg = NULL,   downsample = 0,   timeout = 60,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: limits Numeric vector length 4 giving bounding box decimal degrees c(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax). source Character. WCS source query. One "emodnet" (European waters, high-res) "etopo" (global, 1 arc-minute). Partial matching applies. coverage Character. Override default coverage source (e.g. "emodnet__mean_2022" 2022-vintage EMODnet DTM). NULL uses source default. cache_dir Character. Directory downloaded GeoTIFFs cached. Defaults getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath") tempdir(). force Logical. TRUE, re-download even cached file exists. max_area_deg2 Numeric. Maximum bounding-box area (degree-squared) allowed function errors. Guards accidentally downloading large area. NULL uses source default (50 EMODnet, 2000 ETOPO). Bounding boxes larger source's single-request cap split tiles mosaicked automatically – see Details. tile_size_deg Numeric. Edge length (degrees) largest single-request tile. NULL uses source default (3 EMODnet, 30 ETOPO). EMODnet reads 8-byte doubles internally 4-degree tile already exceeds ~98 MB read cap; ETOPO much coarser larger tiles fine. downsample Integer. Number grid cells skip reducing raster download. 0 (default) keeps native ~115 m resolution; 1 keeps every second cell (~230 m); n keeps every (n+1)-th cell. Applied client-side via st_downsample – server still ships native data (WCS-side resampling honoured read cap), bandwidth saving resulting object's -memory size, download . Useful wider maps native resolution overkill screen rendering. timeout Numeric. HTTP timeout seconds. verbose Logical. Print download progress informational messages.

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: bathyRaster object: list elements raster   (read_stars object positive depth values)   depth.invervals (length-2 numeric range).

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: EMODnet serves European-waters bathymetric DTM   ~115 m native resolution (~0.00104 deg) EPSG:4326 GeoTIFF format.   1x1 deg tile around North Sea ~4 MB; 5x5 deg tile   ~100 MB hit server's ~98 MB read cap. Coverage European   regional seas (~-36 43 lon, ~15 90 lat). ETOPO (ETOPO1 Ice Surface, served NOAA NCEI) global   1 arc-minute (~1.85 km) topo-bathy grid EPSG:4326. Useful EMODnet   coverage. NCEI returns GeoTIFF inside multipart/related MIME   envelope; wcs_bathymetry() extracts binary part transparently. handle bounding boxes larger source's single-request cap,   wcs_bathymetry() splits request tiles   tile_size_deg per axis, caches tile, mosaicks via   GDAL virtual raster. tile cached independently subsequent   overlapping requests reuse already disk. returned object bathyRaster (class returned   raster_bathymetry depths = NULL) can   slotted directly basemap(shapefiles = list(bathy = ...))   passed vector_bathymetry. Citation requirements: EMODnet bathymetry published CC-  must cited used figures   (https://emodnet.ec.europa.eu/en/bathymetry). ETOPO1 also requires   citation (Amante & Eakins 2009, NOAA NGDC; see   https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/etopo-global-relief-model).

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
text: Mikko Vihtakari

title: Download bathymetry from a WCS endpoint — wcs_bathymetry
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   # European waters, high resolution   bathy <- wcs_bathymetry(c(2, 3, 54, 55), source = "emodnet")   basemap(c(2, 3, 54, 55),           shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL,                             bathy = bathy$raster),           bathymetry = TRUE)    # Global coverage (Hawaii -- outside EMODnet)   bathy <- wcs_bathymetry(c(-160, -154, 18, 23), source = "etopo")   basemap(c(-160, -154, 18, 23),           shapefiles = list(land = dd_land, glacier = NULL,                             bathy = bathy$raster),           bathymetry = TRUE) } # }

title: ggOceanMaps 2.4 (development version on GitHub)
text: EMODnet — bathy.style = "wcs_emodnet_blues" ("wemb") / "wcs_emodnet_grays" ("wemg"). ~115 m European-waters bathymetry. ETOPO1 NOAA NCEI — bathy.style = "wcs_etopo_blues" ("wceb") / "wcs_etopo_grays" ("wceg"). ~1.85 km global topo-bathy. Use EMODnet coverage region. Bounding boxes outside source’s coverage error cleanly pointer right alternative. Large boxes tiled mosaicked automatically. Tiles cached getOption("ggOceanMaps.datapath"). vector_land() extracts land polygon bathyRaster produced raster_bathymetry(). Pairs vector_bathymetry() build--shapefile workflows. New AGENTS.md AI assistants helping users use ggOceanMaps, new vignette("cookbook") short copy-pasteable recipes. New vignette("bathymetry") covering bathymetry sources, new vignette("adding-graphical-elements") covering ocean-current arrows (velocity quivers schematic “Figure 1” arrows) pie charts maps via scatterpie::geom_scatterpie().
